item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how this fish species isn't actually really anomalous, nothing about it breaks the laws of physics or evolutionary biology as we know them. It's the Foundation's purview not because it is actually anomalous in any real sense, but because it threatens humanity in much the same manner as real anomalies.

This article is very interesting and well written on it's own, but it's even more interesting in the way it explores the limits of what constitutes an anomaly.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image comes from Wikimedia Commons and the second image was made for the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In regard to the population explosion of 1238 I would suggest something other than decline in predation due to overfishing and its impact on said predators. If the fish are as toxic as suggested (particularly due to heavy metals) the only things eating them would have to be specialised to that diet. As such the mention of cetaceans doesn't really work. As a possible solution that paragraph at the start of the analysis could suggest that the marked change in population was due to changes in reproductive cycle or even reproductive physiology. This could be linked to human pollution of the deep ocean with various heavy metals not commonly found in the area or the like. These pollutants would aggregate in the silt and on rock formations in the Benthic layer and could definitely cause this change. Otherwise quite an interesting article. This one bit tipped me from an up vote to a no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a little something I wrote for And Then I Died IV - Series 2 before deciding it was too vague with regards to the SCP in question and choosing a different one. Enjoy!

They say that no man is an island, but I'm not entirely sure if that's actually the case. At least, that's how it's felt for the couple of days.

That's how long it's been since the floodwaters came and washed down my town. I really wasn't expecting that, given how far inland we were. Spokane is mighty far away from the ocean, and it seems the flood further. but I had been listening to the radio and knew it was coming. Grabbed as much as I could and hopped in the boat I had in my backyard. Worked out well enough for me, but I'm starting to run out of supplies out here.

Food? Running out? Clean water? Ran out days ago. Gas? Only started with an hour or two, at most. Fishing supplies? Wasted it all by now. Sunscreen? If I survive this (unlikely), I will be more bronze than a Greek God.

I keep listening on the radio, trying to make out any sign of hope. Small bits got through the static, lit bits and chunks: "…dation. We…" "…K-Class….of global…" "…three-eight-muth…" "…rescue…unlikely…". So I can guess that society is still out there, in some respects, at least. Can't count on their help, but I'm not the only person alive.

So here I am, Noah on my ark. I don't think I've got the divine help of God on myself, unlike the other guy. Case in point: Three days without water is the saying. It's been four. My lips are cracked, my throat sore. I'm too dizzy to move, so I'm just slumped next to the wheel of the boat.

And then I died.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the more horrifyingly plausible and realistic SCPs I've read. One suggestion I'd give is explaining more graphically how the disaster will affect the rest of the world (like the tsunamis destroying countries in Asia that ScienceBoink mentioned). In any case, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally finish reading this, +1.

The anomalous is blend and almost boring. On one hand, that's why it take me so long to finish reading the article. On the other hand, it's much easier to suspend disbelief, and thus the impact of the whole K scenario seem very realistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but that picture is just so creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay. So you write that the things give birth to 40,000 eggs, of which 25-35% eventually grow to adulthood, let's assume the low end- 25%. Let's say that just 1 in 5 of those adults reproduce (even a figure that low would be rare on a species with characteristics like this) and of course 1/2 is female. That means that the average fish generates 1/2*1/5*1/4*40,000 which in turn reproduce. That's one thousand per fish.

If I'm not mistaken that means that the entire mass of planet should have become tunnel fish within ten years max if I'm not mistaken. Since this is impossible, it would tend to imply that the fish have recently (within the last 1-2 years) undergone a massive spike in reproductive success, but that's inconsistent with the text which suggests that their current reproductive success is indicative of longer term processes.

In other words, exponential growth fucks it up.

Otherwise brillant. One of the top 50 SCP's on the site. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna feel reely gillty about this later, but the opportunaty to make some kriller puns is always a whalecome occurrence.

Ofishially I'd say that this one is dolphinately crayative. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote this for clinical tone and the note, but I really can't. There is a very big piece missing - what about the tidal wave when Oregon collapses into the ocean? Japan, China, etc have almost the same level of risk but aren't mentioned in the SCP. Also, there needs to be at least a reference to a cover story to be given to seismologists (cave networks, something about fault lines?) because they will see the holes.

I get the feeling that this is an example of an SCP written early in the containment attempt. If you looked at a version from 20 - 50 years in the future, you would see that the Foundation had eventually found the materials and technology to properly contain this. 1. A 'paint' to make that stretch of rock taste nasty (similar to sharks and undersea cables). 2. Some sort of fast and largescale method to block off the openings. 3. Something pumped into the tunnels to just kill anything inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dont we have an scp that can fill the holes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
O5-8? Isn't he married to SCP-500-J?

Also: Oh, so THAT'S what happened in 2012(the movie). The world got eaten by fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There isn't anything anomalous about it

1) Only 10% of the volume of the rock they eat gets excreted out, and they aren't bulking-up from the rock they eat, so a lot of the matter they're eating is just somehow disappearing.

2) They don't just digest rock, they bite chunks of rock out of solid stone. And they do so without the ridiculously oversized jaw-muscles or steel-like teeth that would be necessary to do so under the constraints of physics and biology as we understand them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dangerous part about them is that they ear so much rock that it's destroyin the environment above them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like this. It's a fish that eats rock. There isn't anything anomalous about it so there's no reason the Foundation shouldn't inform world governments and have them do something. Also, how has no one noticed this other than the Foundation? -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had to downvote. The collapse region was what broke the suspension of disbelief for me, and after that this SCP just does not make any sense. Even if the region could stay standing with a cavern as tall as mountains under it the sea level drop from filling the caverns up would be undeniable. Something like this just isn't consistent with the real world. If the tunnels were reduced down to a possible extent then you are just left with a weird fish, no need for foundation involvement at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't think "filling in their tunnels" would be that easy with real-world technology. It took months for BP to seal the Deepwater Horizon oil gusher using state-of-the-art technology, and that was just the matter of plugging a hole less than a few meters wide - and which is STILL seeping oil to this day in spite of the fact that it doesn't have tens of thousands of lithivorous fish chewing on it. Filling in an entire tunnel, hundreds of kilometers long, and underwater, would be an undertaking on par with putting a man on the moon.

You're being a bit generous there, don't you think? Putting someone on the moon is a matter of a few thousand individuals, going down to a couple hundred after the craft has been built. Beyond that, it's relatively easy.

Exterminating a rock-eating ocean floor Keter-Fish? Unless we've got an army of Roosevelts in cryo-storage somewhere, that isn't gonna happen. Mostly because, well, the fish are useless. There's nothing to gain for the effort. …err, other than not losing 1/10th or so the Pacific-American coast. But that's a big, -big- thing. Hard for people to wrap their heads around, even when displayed on a map. Even harder to point out all the side effects of that much shit falling into the ocean. I might be a bit off due to the projection of that map, but I think Russia may need a new port on the Pacific, let alone the sheer balls-out insane death tolls from Tsunamis in East-Asia and Oceania.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personal canon, Boa. Both of those things involve the Foundation throwing away its cover for something that probably won't even work, and I like to think that Smapti's set this in a bleaker world where there's no escape if something happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to suspend the hell out of my disbelief for this one, but it was worth it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't feel like these fish are weird enough to be an item. I know that they are but they just don't feel anomalous. To me, this would fit better as a disaster scenario planning document, something like project heimdal, than as a main list scp.

A scenario: there is an information breach and full knowledge of the fish and the tunnels gets out. Most people chalk it up to one more potential-disaster-that-could-kill-us-all and go about their business, just like they do with global warming, biological warfare, nuclear warfare, and ecosystem collapse. A very small percentage of people, mostly those with a direct vested interest in the Gold Zone, start a major campaing to get the government to "do something". Best case scenario, they introduce a predator or a pathogen that reduces the population. Worst case, all plans utterly fail and nothing changes. Either way, in the long term people continue on with their lives on the edge of disaster just like the residents of Pompei. There is no threat to normalcy, because looming disasters are normalcy in the modern world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm definitely giving this thing a +1; it's incredibly well written, it has a demonstrable effect on the environment around it, we know how we can TRY to contain it, and exactly what will happen if we can't.

That said… It's just not very anomalous. It's a fish that eats rocks and digs tunnels to spawn in, neither of which are COMPLETELY unheard of. The only anomalous aspects are that sometimes it eats only other fish and sometimes it eats exclusively rock; and when it's in rock-eating mode, it poops a fraction of what it eats, laws of conservation of mass be damned.

That said, +1 for fish which may accidentally make half the western seaboard go plop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sampti, Eskobar's comments got me thinking and made me downvote this.

While a rock-eating fish is plausible, undermining the western seaboard of the United States to the extent that a sudden seismic event would cause the catastrophic collapse presented here is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really see how the article has a "most important SCP item in existence" vibe. As far as SCPs go, causing the collapse of civilization isn't a particularly dangerous effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's true; parrot fish eat rock and they already exist. The note really does make this article. But I don't know if that kills it for me. I still give it +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. And now I feel a bit less safe knowing how much of our civilization's resources are in a relatively small area like that.

Here's an article: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Megaregions_of_the_United_States

Want to see it on a map? http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/51/MapofEmergingUSMegaregions.png

Those are the areas of the US you need to destroy to really fuck shit up. Any one of them would do.

And they're not exactly large.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. And now I feel a bit less safe knowing how much of our civilization's resources are in a relatively small area like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. What I enjoy about this one is that it's not particularly noteworthy in isolation: It eats rocks, sure, but there's some weird-ass species of slug that does that too, and anyways who cares? This is what it is because of humanity's interaction with its habitat, and where that idea usually goes into ~personal hubris~ or some jabberwocky, this actually functions as a structural critique on a basic process of society. Folks gotta eat, and the subsequent ecological displacement reveals an underlying strangeness in nature. I like the idea of humanity's population explosion being something of an anomalous effect in itself; this is an SCP because of the shadow cast by human patterns of movement, and the negative space of the article is all about how weird that is. It's an idea that's been explored elsewhere on the site, I'm sure, but this executes it well.

There's also a pleasing mythic feel about it. It's a fish that eats the land. You could read the species as a sort of aggregate leviathan, a primordial ocean-fear along the lines of Phorcys or any number of kraken mythos. It's got a very deep religious foreboding theme, and the completely banal understatement of, essentially, a goofy-ass looking fish sells it. It hits a strong chord without you even noticing that it has until after.

Anyways, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

For those curious, IK-class events come from In the Big League where it describes a truly massive natural disaster, just like here, (though in the tale, it's not a disaster caused by anomalous means). I always enjoy references like that, and I like the spin on it taken here. Puts it at a bigger scale than originally implied by that tale, and has obvious re-use value.

I do have a couple issues, though.

(1) I don't care for the Mel's Hole reference, though. Too obvious. Like, let's throw Mel's Hole in there because that particular urban legend hasn't been run into the ground! (This partly stems from my history as a script reader circa 2009, when apparently the coolest idea evar was apparently to do a spec script on Mel's Hole and I got so sick of that crap.)
(2) This article essentially presents this SCP as the most important SCP item in existence. I really don't care for that. I mean, this really has to be a gold star triple A article for me to get on board with that. It feels like a contrivance.

I still like the article, and I upvoted it accordingly, but there you have it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've got to say, despite the incredulity a lot of people are feeling about the scenario put forth, there's a lot to like here. Thank you also for the concept of an IK-class societal collapse scenario, I see some interesting possibilities with that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I might argue that yes, it would. The loss of huge swaths of California farmland is one significant point, but here's the more sobering thought for those of us here present: we'd also be losing San Jose, San Francisco, Paolo Alto, Mountainview, and Seattle in one fell swoop. That's the headquarters of Apple, Microsoft, HP, Google, Amazon, along with hundreds of other tech companies, with all of their servers, resources, and manpower, completely wiped off the map. Several major international airports would be completely gone, and since the traveling-salesman problem is NP-complete and bureaucracies are asnine, I have to imagine it would take ages before air travel recovered enough to be functional through much of the western seaboard. The entertainment industry is fairly well invested up here as well- say goodbye to Pixar, (Dreamworks?), and enough soundstages and the like to leave gaping holes in the TV and Movie lineup. These are just what I can think of off the top of my head.

To summarize some of these specific examples: the economy is going to tank, food prices are going to skyrocket due to the shortage, entertainment is going to be scarce, and to top it all off we'll have blown a hole in the internet the size of Texas. Add that to anything I'm overlooking, and once they get over the initial shock, PEOPLE ARE GOING TO FREAK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keter Fish? Well written Keter Fish?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, you spawned a lot of huge comments. (That was not a fish pun, NO FISH PUNS.)

I'm just gonna say I liked it. Environmental impact of overfishing… Another good article on current events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Surely all of the water that's entered the tunnels would be equivalent to what would be covering the Golden Zone once the event actually occurs? The way I figure it, the sea level should have already lowered significantly because of all of those tunnels.

Nonetheless, the force and vibrations produced by the vast majority of the Pacific Northwest suddenly sinking hundreds or even thousands of meters (a lot of the area that would be covered in water consists of mountains) would cause some nasty effects all over the world. A tsunami bigger than anything we've seen before; earthquakes all over the planet; countless volcanic eruptions. Yes, Washington, Oregon, and British Columbia would be royally screwed. But much of the rest of the world would be in a pretty tight situation as well. Yeah, I'd say there is a huge risk of a collapse-of-civilization event, but not for the reasons stated in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3. Given that the Foundation has known about this for at least a couple of decades, is there a specific reason why they can't begin a more…informal evacuation of the Gold Zone? Arrange for the cultural artifacts to be relocated under various pretenses? Carry out some terrorist attacks or fake some nuclear meltdowns to discourage human settlement in the area? Just something a little more proactive than "let's wait for shit to go down"?

The Foundation is subtly trying to do so by encouraging the development of a "green" culture that will have a minimal economic impact if it's destroyed, but considering how big the Gold Zone is, and how many people live there, even announcing to the public that they're living on borrowed time wouldn't result in mass relocation. In the real-world northwest, an eruption of Mt. Rainier (which last happened only about 300 years ago and could feasibly happen again this century) will bury thousands of people's homes under dozens of feet of mud, completely sever north-south travel through the Puget Sound region, and force the relocation of probably half a million people. This is a real threat that people are aware of - but despite putting up some warning sirens and a few "Volcano Evacuation Route" signs, it hasn't halted development and population growth in the region. For that matter, if Yellowstone were to erupt, pretty much the entire western US would choke to death on toxic gas, and the impact to civilization would probably be greater than what i've proposed in this SCP, but it hasn't stopped the west coast cities from growing larger and larger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Surely we could produce a tailored poison/explosive and drop it down to them, like that river that they poisoned to kill off the invasive species present (admittedly they fucked up and it was a bad idea but I think we could do a better job with our resources.)

Plus if we make a mistake we could just deliberately collapse the tunnels and pull a "marianas trench"-style reboot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Again I repeat: Who would oppose the decimation of the country sinking fish? No one is making a market from keeping the fish alive and no one would lose their business from killing off these fish.

The people making a market off of the fish that eat those fish, and whose bottom line would be affected by not being able to fish for them.

Moreover, killing off one specific species of fish is a much more difficult proposition than you suggest. We could start just dumping millions of liters of anti-fish poison into the ocean, and turn the entire northern Pacific into a dead zone. Would that be a good idea? Of course not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was very curious what would a SCP with a large fish and a Lvl 4 clearance, classifier Keter, do.

I liked the idea, what I didn't like was - black spaces on a Lvl 4 approved document? I know we are playing into suspense and stuff here, but would a document with so highly rated approval rate needed to be censored?

What I liked was the very tragic scenario, beginning from a few fishes "eating" a bit of land, ending with the collapse of society. Wow. I agree with Scantron that it might not end in this IK class event (although the area is, in fact, a very first-world one), but it wouldn't be a nice event at all.

And, I liked the introduction of another -K class event. There was this thread on the forums where the K class events are discussed and I always like seeing a new one!

And a nice touch with poor O5-8.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would losing that area really have the potential to escalate to a collapse-of-civilization event? I mean, it'd be a massive disaster/tragedy and a bitch and a half to deal with, but I don't get the feeling that it'll snowball into the collapse of civilization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see why this is a foundation matter, despite not being particularly supernatural: If the world knew this was coming, it would cause mass panic. It's not massively anomalous, but it does warrant a coverup to some extent.

However, I'd like to see the foundation doing something more pro-active. Closing up the tunnels is helpful, but not very; the fish will make a new lot elsewhere. Make the tunnels more subtly deadly to the fish, so they come into their ancestral tunnel, and then die. Maybe even stock the tunnels with some of their predators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another thing: seismologists look at how various seismic waves travel through rock to learn things about geology. I'm not a geologist/seismologist, but I think that if enough rock was eaten away that it could cause a collapse like that, it would affect the passage of seismic waves, which means that the readouts of seismographs would have to be doctored to prevent the public from learning about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're comparing area to volume. Comparing volume to volume makes it clear that the collapse could only possibly cause a rise in sea level (or it staying constant) as you're moving material that's above sea level under the water.

Note that a rise in sea level is likely to be far more damaging than a drop, so it's not like it makes the SCP less of a threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for cool: I like the idea of a very for-want-of-a-nail-style SCP, where a small anomaly ("hey, this fish eats rock!") leads to an enormous counterpoint ("hey, Seattle's missing!")

That said, a couple of points:

1. I understand the out-of-universe reason for redacting so many of the numbers in the last addendum (making the effects mysterious but still scary), but there isn't an in-universe reason for hiding any of that information if it were available. The depth to which the Gold Zone would be flooded (triple-digits, though? really?), the extent of the subsequent drop in global sea levels (which I'm still not sure would happen; see above), the projected casualty count, the area of the Gold Zone; all of this is really, really important information, and if an inter-O5 memo is going to be included in a containment document, it would need to include that data.

Also, this is an interactive map details the effects of a global rise in sea level by 60 meters. I know it's not the scenario we're talking about here, but it's still a bit different. Additionally, this is a map that lets you zoom in on a location (like the Pacific Northwest) and check elevation at different points. A lot of the area you have in the Gold Zone actually has a pretty high elevation; the smallest I saw in the Montana/Washington/Idaho/Oregon part was around 300m, and got up to 1200m in a few places. The Imperial Valley, sure. But unless the fish are carving out tunnels that are 1.2km wide, it's hard to figure out the exact placement of the Gold Zone.

2. Just a question. The fish eat rock. What are they pooping? Logically, the rock removed from beneath the Pacific Northwest is being deposited somewhere, Andy Dufresne-style.

3. Given that the Foundation has known about this for at least a couple of decades, is there a specific reason why they can't begin a more…informal evacuation of the Gold Zone? Arrange for the cultural artifacts to be relocated under various pretenses? Carry out some terrorist attacks or fake some nuclear meltdowns to discourage human settlement in the area? Just something a little more proactive than "let's wait for shit to go down"?

4. Aren't there other ways of killing these things en masse? Poisoning the rock? Poisoning or fishing to extinction their other food sources? Just slaughtering them for reasons other than food production? We might not fish for them under normal circumstances, but if they're destroying the land we live on, I'm sure we can find a way to make them extinct.

I still like it, but I had some thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I have a hard time believing that enough rock could have been eaten by these fish since 1850 to cause a problem. Did you do any calculations on the number of fish and the amount of rock involved to determine if it was feasible?

2) Unless most of what the stone that they eat just vanishes, these fish are going to produce as waste "slag" which has almost equal volume to the rock they eat. This would have multiple problems:

a) If the fish crapped in the tunnels they'd block off the tunnels they were making, so they'll have to make round trips to the tunnel entrance to crap. This will limit the length of the tunnels they can make.

b) Considering the huge amounts of rock they eat, they'd create huge piles of slag-crap outside the tunnel entrances. If the piles got high enough they'd threaten to collapse and bury the tunnel entrances, so each year the fish will have to go further and further from the tunnel entrance to take a crap.

c) The vast piles of slag-crap would be noticed by scientists.

SCP-1238 is not suitable for human consumption due to the large concentrations of toxic minerals consumed by the fish, and are not currently fished for in any significant quantity or exploited by human industry for any significant purpose.

This sounds like a species that the general scientific community would be aware of. Further, they'd be trying to figure out what in their diet caused them to have such high concentrations of various poisonous metals in their bodies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, have no real background in geology. But logically, as far as I can see, the volume of earth that moves downward, decreasing its elevation and letting water cover the area above it, would be exactly equivalent to the volume of water within the tunnels that is pushed out. I mean, I can see some settling by the earth falling into the vacuum of the tunnels, but that doesn't make there be more floodable area. And I really can't see a double-digit decrease in global sea levels as a result. Again, my mileage may vary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's pretty neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, there's actually an almost extinct breed of snail that eats rock, but there's less than a hundred of them left in the world that we know about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't, that's their anomalous property, but as I said I wouldn't be all that surprised if they uncovered a species capable of doing just that. I'd probably shrug and say "neat" when someone told me about them because it's not that crazy.

The primary means by which we hope to limit their population at the time is by restricting whaling and fishing for the animals that feed on the tunnelfish. Any restriction on free market enterprise is certain to run afoul of numerous big corporations and their allies on the political right.

Our big plan isn't to go down and kill the fish or pump filler into the tunnels (I'm sure we have various assets at our disposal that would make either task easier) but to say "hey, stop that" to fishermen.

Again I repeat: Who would oppose the decimation of the country sinking fish? No one is making a market from keeping the fish alive and no one would lose their business from killing off these fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
they're fish with no astounding or insane properties aside from eating rocks.

Name a species of animal capable of digesting rocks.

There really wouldn't be any reason not to consider or believe that these fish exist, is there a big corporation out there that is heavily invested in the survival of the rock munchers? The most opposition I could see against killing these fish would be the wildlife hippy crowds

The primary means by which we hope to limit their population at the time is by restricting whaling and fishing for the animals that feed on the tunnelfish. Any restriction on free market enterprise is certain to run afoul of numerous big corporations and their allies on the political right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
because of an entity that shouldn't logically exist.

I'd agree if these weren't fish, maybe fish made of lava or dragons made of interstellar radiation, but they're fish with no astounding or insane properties aside from eating rocks. These are pretty much the most mundane SCP ever created and if they actually existed I wouldn't be all that surprised.

Don't they? Politics operates in a world where science and evidence aren't nearly as important as emotions and gut feelings.

I agree to an extent, some things aren't given their due respect because the big businesses can buy opinions/votes. There really wouldn't be any reason not to consider or believe that these fish exist, is there a big corporation out there that is heavily invested in the survival of the rock munchers? The most opposition I could see against killing these fish would be the wildlife hippy crowds (who have very little political pull) and even then their opinion is trumped by the threat to american life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't they? Politics operates in a world where science and evidence aren't nearly as important as emotions and gut feelings. That's why the Foundation operates apart from and independent of government - because a government as divided, conservative, and slow to act as ours isn't going to be able to render any meaningful assistance even in the face of blatant evidence that a major chunk of the nation is in danger of annihilation because of an entity that shouldn't logically exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You keep going back to Global Warming but the two aren't comparable, one is a semi-debated theory about man-made gases screwing with the world while these are fish. We could catch a couple and throw them at peoples faces, record them eating rock, explore the created tunnels, and just in general physically prove they exist beyond any reasonable doubt.

Creationists are morons and are pretty laughable in general, no one respects their opinion but religious heavy states and backwater cousin kissers, they don't apply in this situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much the same way they "disprove" the existence of global warming, evolution, abiogenesis, and the Big Bang, I'd suspect - by appealing to people's "common sense" and idealogical forepinnings rather than the science of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love to see how they would disprove the existence of billions of fish and tunnels that are "hundreds of kilometers long".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Risking exposure of the Foundation's existence is not something the O5s are willing to consider when precedent indicates that a major party in the nation at greatest threat from the collapse is more likely than not to claim that the threat doesn't exist at all.

As far as putting a man on the Moon - it took a little over 300 years from when Cyrano de Bergerac first proposed sending a man to the Moon on a rocket, to when it actually happened for real. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair the BP fiasco was an astronomical blunder, back to the fish it's a damn good thing we did put a man on the moon, it didn't take us 100+ years either (unless I'm remembering it wrong.)

Would it be a challenge? Sure, but we could handle it before things get critical and keeping the problem a secret when it affects the global populace is an idiotic choice. Who cares if the politicians start squabbling? We would still have Foundation resources working on the problem and we'd get some support from the international community.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't think "filling in their tunnels" would be that easy with real-world technology. It took months for BP to seal the Deepwater Horizon oil gusher using state-of-the-art technology, and that was just the matter of plugging a hole less than a few meters wide - and which is STILL seeping oil to this day in spite of the fact that it doesn't have tens of thousands of lithivorous fish chewing on it. Filling in an entire tunnel, hundreds of kilometers long, and underwater, would be an undertaking on par with putting a man on the moon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not what I meant, what I'm saying is is that if the news and everyone was alerted to these fish that we could just go in and kill them off, perhaps repair some damage or at least fill in the tunnels. These fish aren't anymore abnormal than their close relatives (aside from eating rocks) so I assume we can just kill them as easily as we kill anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The volume of the water displaced from the tunnels isn't nearly equivalent to the area of land that's ending up underwater, though, and the solid material now occupies less volume due to the massive seismic effects exhibited during the collapse. I'll admit to not being a geologist or a mathemetician, but the root idea of it is that the world's oceans cover a larger surface area post-collapse than they did previously - and as such, the global sea level drops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just love this, Smapti. This would make a brilliant document drop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still not sure about the sea level thing. Almost all of North America is above sea level at the moment, isn't it? So when it collapses downwards, surely there won't be anywhere for the water displaced from the tunnels to go but upwards. Whether this water flows inland in a flood or stays at sea, isn't it going to be higher than it was?
